methodology_note: Revenue paths are derived from sourced driver identities, not hand-drawn fades. AWS: pool (cloud IaaS/PaaS TAM) x AWS market share. Advertising: total digital ad market x Amazon share. Retail: US ecommerce market growth x Amazon share trajectory. Each path's implied ceiling is checked against sourced third-party forecasts. Sources cited inline.

AWS.growth_argument: IDENTITY: AWS Revenue = (Global Cloud IaaS/PaaS TAM) x (AWS Market Share). The global cloud infrastructure market was ~$295bn in 2025 (Precedence Research) and is growing at ~18-20% CAGR driven by AI infrastructure demand, suggesting a TAM of ~$350bn in 2026, ~$415bn in 2027, ~$485bn in 2028. AWS held ~30-31% share in Q1 2026 (businesstats.com), up from the ~32-33% it held in 2022-2023 before Azure accelerated. The BASE assumes AWS holds ~30% share through 2028 as Azure (currently growing faster at ~40% vs AWS at 28%) slowly gains 1-2pp share per year, consistent with historical share shift dynamics in IaaS. The resulting AWS revenue math: $350bn x 30% = $105bn incremental (vs $295bn x 30% = $89bn today); plus AI-specific workloads not fully captured in traditional IaaS TAM estimates add a premium. Ceiling check: $230bn AWS revenue in FY2028 implies AWS holds ~28% of a $500bn+ cloud TAM — within the range of third-party forecasts and below the backlog-implied ceiling. The $364bn backlog (Q1 2026, company-filed) at 80% conversion over 36 months implies ~$97bn of contracted incremental AWS revenue across 2026-2028 alone, before new bookings. That contracted figure supports the base revenue path as a SIGNED commitment floor, not a projection. Source: Amazon Q1 2026 earnings release; Precedence Research cloud infrastructure TAM; businesstats.com cloud market share.

growth_trajectory.rationale: Base deceleration from 28% (Q1 2026) toward 17% by FY2028 reflects logistic maturation: (a) the initial AI training-infrastructure wave converts the existing $364bn backlog over 36 months; (b) as inference and application workloads layer on, they carry better pricing discipline but also more competition from GCP/Azure; (c) historical AWS deceleration phases (2022: 29% to 2023: 13%) show growth can compress rapidly when a capex cycle completes, and the 2026-2028 cycle is the largest ever; (d) the base decline to ~17% by FY2028 is intentionally conservative relative to the bull case but materially above the bear floor, reflecting the contracted backlog providing a visibility floor.

AWS.margin_argument: AWS margin was 35.4% in FY2025 (from $45.6bn OI on $128.7bn revenue) and 37.7% in Q1 2026 ($14.2bn OI on $37.6bn revenue). The Q1 2026 margin is above FY2025 level, confirming strong operating leverage as existing infrastructure is monetized. The normalization toward 36% by FY2028 (vs the Q1 2026 high of 37.7%) reflects: (a) the $200bn 2026 capex will expand depreciation charges over 5-7 years as data centers come online; (b) Trainium custom silicon improves gross margins at the workload level but increases R&D and amortization; (c) pricing competition from Azure and GCP will compress unit revenue per compute hour modestly. The 36% normalized margin sits between the current 37.7% (likely a local peak as new capacity is not yet fully depreciated) and the 2024 floor of 37%+ (which benefited from a period of high utilization on older, depreciated infrastructure). At $230bn normalized revenue, 36% margin yields $82.8bn normalized EBIT.
growth_drivers: AI training and inference workloads: Trainium custom silicon now at $20bn+ run rate growing triple digits; $225bn in committed Trainium purchase obligations. Source: TIKR.com, Amazon Q1 2026 earnings.

Contract backlog conversion: $364bn backlog at Q1 2026 (up from $244bn in Q4 2025), representing ~2.4x current ARR; historical 80%+ conversion over 36 months provides contracted revenue floor. Source: heygotrade.com/en/news/amazon-q1-aws-backlog.

Anthropic and foundation model ecosystem: $100bn+ Anthropic 10-year AWS spend commitment; Bedrock platform aggregating >100 third-party models driving application-layer workloads. Source: CNBC April 2026.
Enterprise AI application layer: shift from model training to agentic/inference workloads broadens the AWS customer base beyond hyperscale AI labs to enterprise software companies.

Geographic expansion: Sovereign cloud driving $80bn in IaaS spend in 2026 alone (Gartner); AWS is positioned as preferred sovereign cloud provider in multiple government programs including $50bn US federal AI infrastructure commitment.
AWS.competitive_moat: AWS holds ~30-31% global cloud infrastructure share, leading Azure (~25%) and GCP (~13%). The moat rests on: (1) NETWORK DEPTH — the broadest service catalog (200+ services) and deepest enterprise integration, creating multi-year customer lock-in via data gravity and toolchain dependency; (2) SILICON DIFFERENTIATION — Trainium and Graviton custom chips provide cost advantages of 30-40% per workload versus comparable NVIDIA-based instances, deepening lock-in for AI customers who optimize against Amazon's hardware; (3) BACKLOG SCALE — $364bn backlog provides unmatched revenue visibility; (4) GEOGRAPHIC REACH — more regions and availability zones than any competitor, critical for enterprise compliance and latency requirements. The share loss concern is real (Azure growing faster at ~40% vs AWS at 28%) but Azure's faster growth is concentrated in Microsoft 365/Teams enterprise customers migrating AI workloads — a different customer cohort than AWS's native cloud-first base. The two are not a zero-sum share war; AI infrastructure spending is expanding the total pool faster than any one provider can absorb.
sources: Amazon Q1 2026 earnings release: $37.6bn AWS revenue, 28% growth, $14.2bn OI, $364bn backlog
Amazon FY2025 annual: $128.7bn AWS revenue, $45.6bn OI (35.4% margin)
Precedence Research: cloud infrastructure TAM $295bn (2025), growing 18-20% CAGR to 2030

fy2025_quarterly.note: Q3 not separately found; implied ~$17.7bn from annual total minus known quarters

Advertising.growth_argument: IDENTITY: Amazon Advertising Revenue = (US digital ad market) x (Amazon share). The US digital ad market is ~$350bn in 2026 (eMarketer/Statista) growing ~12% annually. Amazon holds ~9% of GLOBAL digital ad spend (eMarketer projects 9% for 2026, up from 8% in 2024) and ~15%+ of US digital ad spend given the disproportionate US revenue concentration. Amazon's ad revenue grew 22% in FY2025 to $68.6bn and 24% in Q1 2026 to $17.2bn ($70bn+ TTM). The BASE assumes continued ~22-24% CAGR through FY2026 ($83.8bn) moderating to ~18-20% in FY2027 ($102bn) as Prime Video advertising ramps (contributing 'meaningfully' per Q4 2025 management commentary) but the rate of share gain from Google and Meta stabilizes. Ceiling check: Amazon at $102bn in FY2027 implies ~9.5-10% of a $1.05tn global digital ad market — consistent with eMarketer's trajectory of Amazon growing from 9% to 9.5%+ share. BELOW the ceiling: Amazon at $102bn would still be well below Meta ($243bn) and Google ($239bn), confirming arithmetic plausibility. Source: eMarketer global digital ad projections; Amazon Q1 2026 earnings; Marketplace Pulse advertising revenue series.

growth_trajectory.rationale: Sustained 22-24% driven by: (1) Prime Video ads scaling from near-zero in 2024 to a structurally meaningful ad format; (2) retail media network expanding beyond Amazon.com to DSP/off-site placements; (3) sponsored ads taking wallet from Google search as retail queries move to Amazon. Deceleration to ~20% by FY2027 reflects size effects and gradual Google/Meta response.

Advertising.margin_argument: Amazon does not disclose advertising as a separate P&L segment (it is embedded in North America and International). The 55% estimated contribution margin is derived from: (a) Alphabet's Google Advertising segment runs at ~35-40% operating margin but includes substantial content acquisition costs; Amazon advertising is primarily on-platform sponsored listings with near-zero marginal content cost, supporting a structurally higher margin; (b) Meta's Family of Apps advertising runs at ~55-60% operating margin; Amazon advertising is structurally comparable as a pure digital placement business; (c) the North America segment's operating margin expanded from 6.4% to 6.9% as advertising grew to ~18% of NA revenue, implying advertising is the primary source of segment OI expansion. The 55% is a DERIVED estimate with tier: theia_bottom_up. Single-source flag: not company-disclosed; treated as directional.
growth_drivers: Prime Video advertising: Amazon began inserting ads into Prime Video in 2024 and management noted 'meaningful' contribution in Q4 2025; this is a new high-CPM format additive to search/sponsored listings. Source: Amazon Q4 2025 earnings recap.
Retail media network expansion: Amazon DSP allowing off-site ad placements (programmatic on third-party inventory) extends the monetizable surface beyond Amazon.com.

Search-to-Amazon shift: product search moving from Google to Amazon (already 50%+ of product searches start on Amazon per industry data), pulling ad dollars from Google Shopping toward Amazon Sponsored Products.
Global digital ad market growth: global digital ad spend projected $82bn for Amazon alone by FY2026 per eMarketer (up from 9% share of $916bn = ~$82bn), confirming our bottom-up estimate.
sources: Amazon FY2025: $68.6bn advertising services revenue (+22% YoY)
eMarketer: Amazon 9% global digital ad share 2026, ~$82bn projected
Contribution margin estimate: theia_bottom_up derived from Meta/Alphabet comparables and NA segment margin expansion analysis

NA_Retail.segment_definition: Amazon reports North America as a unified segment including online stores, physical stores, third-party seller services, Prime subscriptions, and advertising services all consolidated. For SOTP purposes this is treated as the 'retail operating system' — the marketplace + logistics + Prime flywheel.
NA_Retail.growth_argument: IDENTITY: NA Retail Revenue growth tracks US ecommerce market growth (~10-12% annually) with modest share gain as Amazon compounds from ~35.7% US ecommerce share (2025 per synctify.net). Q1 2026 NA revenue grew 12% YoY to $104.1bn, consistent with FY2025's 10% full-year pace. The BASE assumes 11% growth in FY2026 and FY2027 as Prime Day (now confirmed for Q2 2026), AI-powered shopping tools (Rufus), and continued 3P marketplace growth maintain above-market share. Ceiling check: $519bn NA segment revenue in FY2027 implies ~37% US ecommerce share of a ~$1.4tn market — consistent with Amazon's share trajectory. Source: synctify.net/2026 US ecommerce market share report; Amazon Q1 2026 earnings.
NA_Retail.margin_argument: North America segment operating margin: FY2022 0.2%, FY2023 4.2%, FY2024 6.5% (est from $25bn OI on $387.5bn), FY2025 6.9% ($29.6bn OI on $426.3bn). Q1 2026 reached 7.9% ($8.3bn OI on $104.1bn), the highest on record. The normalization toward 8.5% by FY2027 reflects: (1) fulfillment automation and robotics continue to reduce per-unit costs (management noted robotics improving throughput and lowering variable labor costs in 2025 earnings calls); (2) advertising mix shift (advertising likely ~18% of NA revenue, contributing ~$56bn at 55% contribution margin) continues to dilute lower-margin product revenue; (3) Amazon Leo drone delivery program adds a one-time cost drag (~$1bn per guidance) in 2026 but is margin-accretive at scale. The 8.5% normalized margin is below the Q1 2026 pace of 7.9% run-rate because Q1 typically benefits from post-holiday inventory efficiency, and full-year seasonality and capex drag will soften the margin somewhat. Source: Amazon segment operating income history; futurumgroup Q1 2026 analysis.
NA_Retail.key_competitive_dynamic: Amazon holds 35.7% of US ecommerce (2025) with the next-largest being Shopify at 14% (aggregated) and Walmart at 6.4%. The Temu/Shein threat is primarily concentrated in the low-price apparel and consumable categories; Amazon's response (Haul low-price marketplace) addresses this defensively. Walmart+ has scaled but remains <20% the size of Prime in membership and lacks the content flywheel. The share dynamic is stable: Amazon is not losing share at scale, but the growth rate of share gain is decelerating as the market matures.
NA_Retail.method_note: mature/recovering segment: current earnings x multiple (10-12x EBIT for a logistics/marketplace combination that includes substantial retail-adjacent advertising earnings). Advertising is the swing factor: strip out the embedded advertising EBIT from the NA segment and the pure retail business earns ~4-5% margin, which would warrant a lower multiple; the blended 8.5% margin reflects the structural improvement from advertising mix.
sources: Amazon Q1 2026 earnings: NA $104.1bn revenue, $8.3bn OI (7.9% margin)
Amazon FY2025: NA $426.3bn revenue, $29.6bn OI (6.9% margin)
marketplacepulse.com: Amazon + Shopify ~50% of US ecommerce

International.segment_definition: All non-US retail, marketplace, and subscription revenue. Major geographies: UK, Germany, Japan, India, France, Italy, Spain, Brazil, Australia, Netherlands, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Singapore, Egypt, Mexico, Poland, Sweden.
International.growth_argument: FX-neutral International growth was 10% in FY2025 and 11% in Q1 2026. The BASE assumes 14% reported growth in FY2026 (includes FX tailwind reversal from USD weakness) decelerating to 13% in FY2027 as India and emerging markets (Brazil, Mexico, Middle East) sustain above-average growth offset by more mature EU geographies. Ceiling check: $210bn in FY2027 implies modest share gain in global ecommerce ex-US — plausible given Amazon is sub-scale in most non-US markets. Source: Amazon Q1 2026 earnings; Amazon FY2025 annual results.
International.margin_argument: International reached 2.9% OI margin in FY2025 ($4.7bn OI on $161.9bn revenue), a significant improvement from near-breakeven in FY2022-2023. Q1 2026 showed further improvement to 3.5% ($1.4bn OI on $39.8bn revenue). The path to 4.5% by FY2028 requires: (1) India achieving meaningful operating leverage as GMV crosses $50bn+; (2) European logistics efficiency gains continuing; (3) advertising attachment in international markets growing (currently below the ~18% of NA, so structural upside exists as international advertising scales). The 4.5% normalized margin is well below NA's 8.5% reflecting the lower advertising mix, higher logistics costs in fragmented markets, and ongoing investment. Source: Amazon quarterly earnings reports; company-filed segment data.
International.method_note: subscale/building segment: current earnings x multiple or option value on India scale. At 4.5% normalized margin on $235bn revenue = $10.6bn EBIT, warranting a 10-12x multiple consistent with a maturing international retail operation with embedded advertising upside.
International.key_risk: FX volatility is material: Q1 2026 reported growth was 19% but FX-neutral growth was only 11%, an 8pp FX tailwind. A USD reversal would compress reported margins and revenue. Additionally, Temu and Shopify's international expansion are the primary competitive threats in price-sensitive markets.
sources: Amazon Q1 2026 earnings: International $39.8bn (+19% reported, +11% FX-neutral), $1.4bn OI
Amazon FY2025: International $161.9bn revenue, $4.7bn OI (2.9% margin)

company_total.note_fy2026: AWS $162.2bn + NA Retail $473.0bn + International $185.0bn + Advertising (embedded in NA/International per Amazon's reporting structure, not added again) = $820.2bn. However Amazon reports advertising within the NA and International segments, so the segment sum excludes double-counting. The company revenue total reflects Amazon's disclosed three-segment structure: NA + International + AWS. FY2025 total from bundle: $716.9bn. Q2 2026 guidance: $194-199bn per management, implying FY2026 full-year tracking toward ~$780-800bn.

fy2026e_revenue_per_segments_bn.reconciliation_note: Advertising is NOT a separately reported segment; it is included within NA and International segment revenue. The sum of 3 segments slightly exceeds the consolidated total because segment reporting includes intersegment items. For valuation, advertising is carved out from NA/International as an analytical segment with its own multiple, not double-counted.

normalized_ebit_breakdown.note: NA Retail pure EBIT subtracts the advertising contribution already captured in the Advertising line. This avoids double-counting. NA Retail 'pure' margin of ~4.5% on $473bn = ~$21bn; plus advertising EBIT of $56bn embedded in NA/International = ~$44bn total NA contribution to normalized EBIT.

sources_summary: All segment revenue figures: Amazon quarterly and annual earnings releases (Q1 2026, FY2025, FY2024)
Advertising revenue: Amazon earnings + eMarketer projections
Capex and FCF: Amazon FY2025 annual report; Q1 2026 10-Q data from search synthesis
